coredata.dc:title: Cd adsorption and detection by silver clusters supported on carbon dots surface: A computational study
coredata.dc:creator: Ambrusi, R.E.
Robein, Y.
Pronsato, M.E.
Brizuela, G.
coredata.dc:description: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out to evaluate the interaction between a Cd atom and silver clusters supported on functionalized and non-functionalized carbon dots (Cdots) surfaces modeled by coronene. The stability and electronic structure is systematically studied through energy, density of states and frontier orbitals analysis in order to establish the changes in the electronic structure and the feasibility of Cd adsorption. Additionally, a bonding investigation is also performed with the aim to determine if the Cd-Ag bond occurs and to verify if after this process the silver clusters remain anchored on Cdots surfaces. This study was also complemented through the calculation of chemical descriptors that allow inferring the electron affinity of the systems. Moreover, sensitivity parameters were evaluated with the aim to characterize the capabilities of the composite silver and Cdots structures for their application on the detection of Cd.

head.ce:title: Cd adsorption and detection by silver clusters supported on carbon dots surface: A computational study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out to evaluate the interaction between a Cd atom and silver clusters supported on functionalized and non-functionalized carbon dots (Cdots) surfaces modeled by coronene. The stability and electronic structure is systematically studied through energy, density of states and frontier orbitals analysis in order to establish the changes in the electronic structure and the feasibility of Cd adsorption. Additionally, a bonding investigation is also performed with the aim to determine if the Cd-Ag bond occurs and to verify if after this process the silver clusters remain anchored on Cdots surfaces. This study was also complemented through the calculation of chemical descriptors that allow inferring the electron affinity of the systems. Moreover, sensitivity parameters were evaluated with the aim to characterize the capabilities of the composite silver and Cdots structures for their application on the detection of Cd.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Substances based on cadmium are commonly employed as stabilizers in PVC products, colour pigment, for special alloy production and in rechargeable batteries among other applications [1,2]. Nevertheless, Cd can contaminate the food chain, which constitutes the main way to enter the human body, affecting human tissues and organs [3]. Moreover, this metal is bioaccumulative, in consequence, it storages over a lifetime once it is absorbed by humans [1]. In addition, human health can be damaged indirectly by Cadmium, since it is found in aquatic environments due to the presence of industrial waste [4]. For that reason, the detection of Cd is fundamental to avoid pollution and toxicity.

Carbon dots (Cdots), which are defined as carbon spherical nanostructures with a size less than 10 nm, can be used, among a wide range of applications, for metal ion sensing through the ability to quench efficiently their fluorescence properties [5]. Apart from size, unique physical and chemical properties can be further modified by changing the chemical composition and the arrangement of the atoms [6]. In general, doping with different types of elements allows to selectively tune the desired physicochemical, electronic, band gap, energy and catalytic properties of Cdots [7–12]. In particular, the exceptional electron donor and acceptor properties of Cdots with functionalized surfaces, were employed to modify a carbon electrode to detect heavy metal ions [13].

Recently, carbon paste and glassy carbon modified with silver nanoparticles have been characterized and evaluated for their structure and sensing properties for Cd and Pb heavy metal ions [14–16]. The relevant characteristic in these processes is that Cd and Pb show under potential deposition (UPD) over Ag surfaces [17–20], which is related to a strong affinity between the metals involved, and consequently, it favors the detection of the analyte with a high sensitivity [15]. For that reason, the doping of Cdots with silver clusters could be an excellent starting point for the design of a nanocomposite material with interesting properties for the detection of Cd. On the other hand, there are several studies on the synthesis and characterization of Ag crystallites interacting with Cdots forming hybrid Cdot@Ag nanostructures [21,22]. Therefore, based on these antecedents, it is expected that hybrid Cdot@Ag nanomaterial has a high potential for application in the development of new materials for sensing Cd ions.

Even though there are experimental studies that evaluate silver-modified carbon substrates for their use in the detection of Cd through electrochemical processes [14,16], these works do not analyze Cdots type of structures. As mentioned before, Cdot@Ag nanostructures were previously studied from a theoretical and experimental point of view, nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, there is no experimental or theoretical data regarding the evaluation of this kind of structures for the potential detection of Cd.

Additionally, such a strong interaction between Ag and Cd due to the UPD process [17–19], which was demonstrated in previous works that can involve nano alloys between these metals [23,24], could lead to a significant modification of the electronic structure and stability of these systems. Moreover, because Cdots were used for the detection of heavy metal ions through spectroscopic and electrochemical techniques [13], the properties related with the strong affinity between Cd and Ag can favor both techniques. In the present work, we develop a systematic approach based on Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations to evaluate hybrid Agn/Cdot structures for the detection of Cd. This analysis will be focused on stability, which would favor an electrochemical sensor, and the changes in the electronic structure that could have a relevant contribution to a spectroscopic sensor. The stability, charge distribution, electronics structure, frontier orbitals, energy band gap, work function and bonding were analyzed to evaluate the performance of hybrid Ag@Cdots nanoparticles for sensing Cd ions. A comparison between extended and cluster theoretical approaches was carried out to carefully study the stability and electronic behavior for the different structures analyzed.

A plane-wave basis set and a periodic supercell method were used to perform DFT calculations employing the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [25,26]. For the functional approximation, the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) was applied [27]. To obtain the electronic density the Kohn–Sham (K-S) equations have been solved variationally. Regarding the applied pseudopotentials, a projector- augmented-wave (PAW) method [28,29] was used. The main parameters of the simulation include; a cutoff energy of 500 eV for the expansion of K-S orbitals and a gamma-centered Monkhorst–Pack scheme [30] for the special k-points (KPs) sampling, to solve integrals over the Brillouin zone using a 1 × 1 × 1 KPs grid. The stepped behavior of the electronic states partial occupations at the Fermi level was softened with a Gaussian smearing approach of 0.05 eV dispersion width.

A conjugate gradient algorithm [31], was implemented for the relaxation of the ions positions. In this sense, the supercell total energy minimization is carried out, considering this geometry optimization to be converged, when the energy difference between successive steps is lower than 10−6 eV for the electronic optimization and the forces on each ion are reduced to 0.01 eV °A−1 or lower values. To take into account the van der Waals interactions (vdW), Grimme's DFT-D2 method was introduced to represent them [32].

A supercell of 30 × 30 × 30 Å was used to optimize the isolated coronene structures with and without an epoxy group, as was already developed in previous publications [33,34]. These models were employed to represent Cdots surfaces. The epoxy group was previously used to represent oxidized carbon surfaces like graphene oxide [35]. Despite the fact that carboxylic groups or hydroxyl groups could be added to the carbon surface [35,36], we choose epoxy group to reduce the number of adsorption sites as a first attempt to study these type of structures. Consequently, as was stated in a previous work [37], the epoxy group was selected with the aim to conjugate the oxygen-containing groupsof Cdots surface representation and the adequacy of this group to the aromatic carbon rings of coronene. This simplification was done to approximate the effect that oxygen-containing groups can have in the interaction with silver.

The local stability of the silver cluster with and without Cd, was confirmed by frequency calculation. All frequencies for the most stable sites were real and positive. The frequencies values are reported in Table S1 and Table S2 of the Supplementary Material.

According to this definition, stable adsorption of the Cd on the Cdot@Ag surface is achieved when the adsorption energy is negative. All adsorption energies obtained with Equation (1) were calculated using a periodic supercell with enough vacuum in all directions to keep the structure isolated. are the total energies of the Cd ion adsorbed on the Agn and the Agn isolated clusters respectively. We also consider the effect of the solvent for the most stable systems and cluster size, using the VASPsol package [38,39]. In these calculations, the equilibrium atom positions were the ones obtained with PBE-D2 functional.

The charge distribution before and after the cadmium adsorption was computed using Bader analysis [40] and evaluated using averaged quantities of the electronic charges on selected atoms. An analysis of Density of states (DOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) plots was performed. Furthermore, chemical parameters like the overlap population (OP) and Bond Order (BO) were included in the calculations to shed light and complement the bonding behavior between different atoms with the aid of the DDEC6 approach [41–43]. With this method, a qualitative and successful comparison of pair of atoms bond strength can be performed when they are immersed in the chemical environments belonging to the studied systems.

Additionally, we have performed DFT single-point calculations of the more stable relaxed structures using ORCA program version 4.0 [44]. The electronic optimization was performed using PBE-D2 functional [27,32]. Open shell and close shell can be obtained depending on the number of silver atoms in the system. For close shell structures, we used the Restricted Kohn Sham approach (RKS) and the minimal multiplicity (m = 1) corresponding to a spin number S = 0. For open shell structures obtained for odd number of silver atoms, we used the Unrestricted Kohn Sham method (UKS) with the lowest spin multiplicity (m = 1) corresponding to S = 1. The self-consistent field (SCF) was obtained with a numerically robust and accurate grid (defgrid2) employing the LANL2DZ basis set [45–48] with a def2/J auxiliary basis set [49]. The convergence criteria were set to a tolerance of 1 × 10−06 Eh (energy change), Last MAX-Density change of 1 × 10−05, Last RMS-Density change of 1 × 10−06 and Last DIIS Error of 1 × 10−06. For Ag and Cd atoms, 28 and 36 electrons respectively were represented by Effective Core Potentials (ECPs) [45–48].

In addition, a Mulliken [50] population analysis was performed to obtain atomic charges and atomic orbitals composition, which was complemented when necessary with a Lӧwdin population analysis [51]. Moreover, Mulliken and Lӧwdin bond orders, indicated as BO_M and BO_L respectively, were calculated to understand the main interactions.

Inaccuracy for the adsorption energy calculations is possible due to the use of a finite basis set. This is known as basis set superposition errors (BSSE) [52], which can lead to an artificial stabilization of the system. Consequently, as an attempt to remove this source of error, we use the counterpoise correction (CP) [53,54]. The BSSE corrections were calculated by the following equations,

Also, an analysis of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) was performed for both; the extended and cluster approach. For the latter, the contribution of subsequent levels in the valence and conduction band denoted as HOMO-1 and LUMO + 1, were added. These states were represented through isosurfaces.

To evaluate the stability and geometry of Cd/Agn/Cdots structures, on functionalized and non-functionalized surfaces, the binding energies and most relevant bond distances were analyzed. Different adsorption sites for the silver clusters Agn (n = 1–3) were tested and the energy was minimized, relaxing all the atoms and obtaining the most stable site for each system. Based on previous work [37], a coronene molecule is used to represent the non-functionalized carbon surface (Cor), while a coronene molecule with an epoxy oxygenated functional group is employed to represent the functionalized Cdot surface (E-Cor). In this work, to obtain the most stable configurations (lower energy) from full relaxations, we analyzed the bridge, hollow and top sites of non-functionalized coronene center hexagonal carbon ring for each cluster size. For Ag clusters with two and three atoms we also considered different orientations; approaching the site with one of their vertices or bonds. In the case of the functionalized coronene, each cluster was located close to the epoxy group. The sites considered were on the top and side of O atom, following the mentioned orientation criterion for the silver dimer and trimer. In the present work, we analyzed the Cd bridge and vertex sites for the silver cluster. The Cd adsorbed on most stable sites; vertex and bridge for Ag2 and Ag3 respectively on Cdot non-functionalized, are shown in Fig. 1 . Similarly, the most stable Cd adsorption sites for the functionalized Cdot, are shown in Fig. 2 ; bridge site for Ag2 and Ag3.

As observed in Figs. 1 and 2, the Cd-Ag bond distance is similar independently of the interaction with a silver cluster. Additionally, the bond distance is of the order of the Cd/Agn isolated cluster, which is between 2.73 and 2.85 Å. Moreover, the bond distances are the same for the systems Cd/Agn/Cor and Cd/Agn; being 2.78 Å, 2.77 Å and 2.85 Å for Cd/Ag1, Cd(v)/Ag2 and Cd(b)/Ag3 isolated clusters. However, the distance from Ag to the C substrate is larger than 3 Å for the non-functionalized surface, while the bond distance is reduced to 2.1–2.7 Å when the silver cluster is attached through an epoxy group to the substrate. The large distance between the CdAgn clusters and the carbon surface makes them similar to the geometrical features of isolated clusters.

Table 1 lists the adsorption energies for Cd on Agn/Cor and Agn/E-Cor, for the vertex (v) and bridge (b) sites. Also, the adsorption energies of Cd on isolated clusters were added as a reference. Additionally, the adsorption energies with counterpoise correction (
E
ads
CP
) were included for the most stable systems, indicated in parenthesis for such cases, to compare both approaches.

It is observed that all sites and sizes are stable for Cd/Agn/Cor. For Ag2 there is a preference for the vertex site, while for Ag3 the bridge site is the most stable. Based on the value of the adsorption energies, a chemical mechanism dominates the Cd binding to the Agn/Cor. Only for the Ag2(b)/Cor system there is an important physical contribution to Cd adsorption, being the less stable adsorption. Nevertheless, the similarity with Cd/Agn isolated systems indicates that the silver cluster is not well attached to the substrate. When the substrate is functionalized with an epoxy group, not all the sites and sizes are stable. As it can be observed in Table 2 , for Ag2 and Ag3 only the bridge site is stable. Additionally, the most stable system corresponds to the one with a single Ag on the surface. Again, the stable sites are dominated by chemical forces. This chemical contribution to the interaction between Cd and Ag would favor the electrochemical detection of Cd ions. The difference in the Cd adsorption energies, indicates that the interaction between silver and oxygen of the epoxy group is meaningful and it is capable of maintaining the metal cluster stable on the support.

The adsorption energies calculated including the solvent effect for the most stable systems, reported in Table S3, show that there are not significant changes in the adsorption energies for the system without the epoxy group. For the functionalized systems, the change in the adsorption energies is larger and the adsorption of Cd on the supported silver trimer could become unstable, but it still keeps the same tendency in stability. The trend follows the same order than in vacuum, being more stable the Cd adsorption on silver clusters of lower size; Cd/Ag1/E-Cor > Cd/Ag2/E-Cor > Cd/Ag3/E-Cor.

There is a high degree of concordance between the values obtained with plane wave functions in a periodic cell and those obtained with LANL2DZ basis functions at the same level, PBE-D2. Most of the differences are around 0.1 eV, and the maximum difference (0.17 eV) is below 0.2 eV which is considered an energy order related to physical interactions and not to electronic contributions [58]. For the isolated clusters the level of coincidence is higher, being exact up to the second decimal place for n = 3 to the vertex site.

A study of the electronic structure was performed to understand at a deep level the Cd adsorption on Agn/Cor and Agn/E-Cor. An analysis of the total density of states (TDOS) and PDOS on Cd and Ag atoms for both hybrid Cd/Agn@Cdot structures, with and without the epoxy group, was carried out. Figs. 3 and 4
show the most important results obtained for the Ag1 cluster size, and Figures S1-S4 (Supplementary Material) for the Agn(n = 2–3) cluster. The results obtained for the Agn@Cdot structures without Cd and the isolated Cd-Agn clusters were included as references. For Cd/Agn@Cdot structures, the results for the most stable site were plotted.

Fig. 3 A) shows the TDOS for the Ag1/Cor system before and after Cd adsorption. The curves present peaks that are in general shifted and the emergence of new ones. This shift in some peaks causes a band gap modification. For the Ag1/Cor system the band gap appears between the unoccupied states corresponding to the peak at approximately 1.19 eV, and the partly filled band corresponding to the peak at the Fermi level, giving a band gap of approximately 1 eV. After interaction with Cd, the conduction band peak is shifted to 0.79 eV, leading to a band gap of approximately 0.6 eV. This change in the band gap causes an increment in the conductivity, which is important, especially in the formation of a new useful material for the detection of Cd.

PDOS curves (Fig. 3C)) show that Cd and Ag orbitals overlap at approximately −3.97 eV, −2.55 eV and at the Fermi level. Compared with the isolated system (Fig. B), a different behavior is observed. The peaks at −2.55 and −3.97 eV are at slightly higher energies when the cluster is isolated, being more stable when the cluster is supported. Additionally, there are changes in the peak positions of the conduction band. However, there is no overlapped states peak at 0.79 eV to explain the shifted peak observed at TDOS. It seems, this peak is related to the substrate states and the shift from 1.19 eV to 0.79 eV in the conduction band, is induced by the interaction of the substrate with the CdAg1 cluster.

The same analysis was performed for the Ag1 monomer supported on the E-Cor substrate (Fig. 4). There is also a shift of some states to lower energies in the Cd/Ag1/E-Cor compared to Ag1/E-Cor systems. However, it is not as significant as in the case of Ag monomer supported on Coronene. The major differences occur at energies below −3 eV. There is still a partially filled band at the Fermi level, and the peak at 0.5 eV is shifted to 0.4 eV in the conduction band, after the Cd adsorption. This change leads again to a reduction of the band gap from 0.4 to 0.2 eV. PDOS curves (Fig. 4c)) show more overlapped states between Cd and Ag atoms than on the Cd/Ag1/Cor system, which can explain the higher stability of the Cd/Ag1/E-Cor system (more negative adsorption energy). There are unoccupied overlapped states between Cd and Ag at 0.4 eV. Consequently, the peak at 0.4 eV which in the TDOS determines the band gap when Cd is adsorbed, can be related this time to the direct influence of the Cd and Ag interactions.

As the cluster size increases, similar behaviors can be appreciated for the different systems. The band gap of Cd(v)/Ag2/Cor is reduced again due to the shifting of the peak from 1.72 to 1.36 eV after the Cd adsorption. From PDOS curves, it can be observed that this change is in part directly related to overlap between Cd and Ag states. The band gap reduction from 1.66 to 1.46 eV after the Cd adsorption occurs due to the Cd and Ag interaction. For the Cd(b)/Ag2/E-Cor system, despite some changes in TDOS after the Cd adsorption, the states related to the band gap (the peak at −0.3 eV in the valence band and the peak at 0.54 eV in the conduction band) are less influenced by the presence of Cd. Nevertheless, there is still a reduction in the band gap from 0.54 to 0.42 eV approximately after the Cd adsorption, which is around 0.1 eV lower than the band gap modification observed in the not functionalized system. In part, this could be again due to the change in the conduction band around the Fermi level and not directly related to interaction of Ag and Cd, but from changes in substrate states induced by this interaction. Additionally, as was stated before, an increment of the Ag and Cd overlapped states at the valence band is observed when Cd(v)/Ag2/Cor and Cd(b)/Ag2/E-Cor systems are compared, which explains the higher stability of the latter system.

For Cd(b)/Ag3/Cor the band gap increases by 0.2 eV, since the peak at the Fermi level remains unchanged after the Cd adsorption, but the peak of the conduction band at 0.55 eV shifts to 0.65 eV when Cd is adsorbed. This leads to a change in the band gap from 0.4 eV to 0.6 eV after the doping with Cd. For Cd(b)/Ag3/E-Cor, the band gap is not modified after Cd adsorption. We believe that this occurs because the geometric structure does not follow the same pattern as the other systems. The O atom belonging to the epoxy group is only bonded to one C atom (see Fig. 2) as a consequence of the interaction with the Ag metal cluster. This geometric modification leads to changes in the electronic properties, for instance, the peak at the Fermi level does not have contributions of the Cd and Ag states according to PDOS curves. Even though, the lower energy conduction band states have the contribution of the Cd and Ag overlapped states, there is no change in the peak positions of the band. Additionally, this system is less stable than the other structures with epoxy functionalized substrate (see Table 1).

The charge distributions through the Bader method in all the systems are shown in Table 2. For each atom, the net charge is obtained by subtracting the number of core electrons and the Bader population to the atomic number. Then these values are averaged for considering all the atoms in the system, and negative values represent the electron excess and positive values electron deficiency of the atoms.

On one hand in the Cd(b)/Ag2/Cor system, the Cd and Ag atoms remain almost neutral, for that reason the binding energy is not very high. On the other hand, Cd/Agn/Cor systems experience a charge transfer from Cd to the Agn/Cot surface, leaving the Cd positively charged. When the Cd adsorbs on silver clusters on the functionalized carbon surface, there is a charge transfer from Cd to the substrate leaving the Cd positive. The O atom remains negatively charged before and after the Cd adsorption [37]. Additionally, C and H atoms of the coronene structure maintain approximately their neutrality with or without a functional group. Ag clusters remain almost neutral and in some cases positive, being stable two of them; Cd/Ag1/E-Cor and Cd(b)/Ag3/E-Cor systems. In general, the charge transfer from Cd to Agn/Cor and Agn/E-Cor substrates is in concordance with more negative adsorption energies, meaning that the charge transference has an important role in the mechanism that stabilizes these systems. Exceptions to this rule are only the Cd(v)/Ag2/E-Cor and Cd(v)/Ag3/E-Cor systems, in which probably other factors are influencing their stability.

Additionally, for the most stable Cd adsorption sites, a population analysis employing the Mulliken method was performed, for specific atoms. The criterion adopted to select the atoms was to choose the Ag atom closer to the Cd atom to account for the Cd and Ag interaction. For the substrate structures we selected the C atom closest to the Ag atom in the cluster in the case of coronene, and for the E-Cor structure we selected the O atom and its closest C atom. The results are summarized in Table 3 .

The same as predicted by the Bader method, for the Cd/Agn/Cor systems, the Mulliken charge for a C atom has a small value, remaining almost neutral as can be observed in Table 3. Additionally, Cd has a positive charge and the Ag cluster negative charge. An exception is the system Cd/Ag1/Cor, for which Cd and Ag are almost neutral. However, this can occur because the Mulliken method has a contribution of half of the charge corresponding to the overlapped contribution between different atoms, and this value could be negative lowering artificially the real charge of the atom. To eliminate this doubt we also compute the Lӧwdin charge obtaining a value of 0.21, −0.32 and −0.01 e for Cd, Ag and C respectively, consistent with the trend observed.

The p contribution on the silver clusters corresponds to the lower sublayer electronic levels because the silver atom was modeled with 19 valence electrons (28 electrons were frozen). The carbon orbital composition does not change after Cd adsorption. This could mean that there is no appreciable interaction between coronene and silver, considering that the C atom also has no appreciable change in its atomic charge. The Cd and Ag atoms keep approximately the same composition of orbital contribution for Cd/Agn/Cor systems for the different silver sizes. The main contribution is between d orbitals, especially for the Cd that is responsible for approximately 85 % of the charge, which is not strange taking into account that its electronic configuration has ten d electrons.

For the Cd/Agn/E-Cor systems, the Cd and Ag atoms do not present an appreciable change in their orbital composition compared to the system without the epoxy group. The O atom has a higher contribution of the p orbitals compared with C in the interaction with silver. C does not change its orbital composition compared with the systems without the epoxy group, but it has a negative charge according to the Mulliken method because of the bonding with the O atom. However, the Lӧwdin and Bader method predicts positive charges for this C atom, being 0.227, 0.324 and 0.527 e according to Bader and 0.103, 0.109 and 0.092 according to Lӧwdin for Cd/Ag1/E-Cor, Cd(b)/Ag2/E-Cor and Cd(b)/Ag3/E-Cor respectively. In the case of O and Cd atoms, the three methods predict the same charge sign, with the O atom always negative and Cd always positive. The Ag charge behavior is in general positive for Bader (0.04–0.29 e) and Mulliken (0.15–0.27 e) approaches and the opposite for the Lӧwdin method being between −0.23 and −0.51 e. For that reason, it is not so easy to establish the charge sign for Ag clusters. Even though the magnitude of the charges depends strongly on the method and basis functions used, it is possible to highlight some trends in the charge distribution as a consequence of the interactions between the metals atoms and the substrate. For example in the adsorption of Cd on Agn/Cor substrates, the Cd atom experiences a charge transfer to the Ag cluster. The charge of Cd after the adsorption is between 0.150 and 0.172 according to the Bader method, while Lӧwdin is between 0.32 and 0.46 e, instead Mulliken again underestimates the charge value for Cd, however, the three methods conclude that it is positively charged. Something similar occurs with O atom; between −0.84-(−0.96) e, −0.18-(−0.36) e and −0.22–(−0.39) e with Bader, Lӧwdin and Mulliken respectively. In the case of the silver atom nearest to Cd, could be negative or positive depending on the method employed.

Even though Cd transfers charge to the Ag cluster, it also interacts with coronene through the O atom so it is difficult to establish the charge balance in the system. Although the O atom increases its negative charge after the interaction with the Cd/Agn cluster, this may be due to due to the charge donated by the Ag cluster which includes also the amount of charge received from the Cd atom.

The Cd interaction with the supported Agn clusters was also analyzed from a chemical point of view. BO and OP calculations were carried out for the most relevant bonds and are listed in Tables 4 and 5
. The atoms that have been taken as reference are indicated in Fig. 5 . Each CC and CO bond of the composite systems was compared with the same bonds in the Cor or E-Cor systems and the Ag-Ag and Cd-Ag bonds in the isolated Agn and Cd/Agn clusters respectively. In addition, the bond distance was included.

In Table 4, the most stable Cd/Agn/Cor systems electronic population for each Agn cluster size, shows that there are not very significant OP and BO values for Ag-C bond, denoting that it is weak. In this sense, these OP and BO values are in the order of the values obtained for the same systems without Cd [37], which means that the silver is physically adsorbed to the carbon substrate, even after Cd adsorption. The Cd atom interacts with the Ag cluster for Cd/Ag2/Cor and Cd/Ag3/Cor with OP and BO values that are only 4 and 6 % lower than in the isolated cluster, which is consistent with the good stability of the adsorption of Cd (see Table 1). Moreover, the values BO_M and BO_L confirm the interaction between Cd and Ag. For Cd/Ag1/Cor, the OP is 35 % lower, but the BO is approximately 60 % higher than the ones for the isolated cluster. For that reason, this system maintains its stability comparable to the Ag cluster with two and three atoms. Moreover, in the case of Ag2 and Ag3, the Ag-Ag interaction reduces its OP and BO values a 7 and 8 % respectively, with respect to the isolated cluster for Ag2, and it increases these parameters a 9 % for Ag3, maintaining in both cases a considerable cohesion. However, the main problem is that the Cd-Ag cluster is not attached very strongly to the substrate.

For the Cd/Agn/E-Cor systems (Table 5), even though Cd-Ag OP and BO values are reduced or increased without any trend for the different cluster sizes, the supported Cd-Ag cluster and the isolated one have OP and BO values that are comparable in magnitude, which contributes to their stability. Again, this trend is supported by the BO_M and BO_L results. Also, Ag-O and CO have significant OP and BO values, maintaining the same order calculated for the systems without Cd [37], which is important to ensure that the Cd-Ag cluster is well attached to the support. Additionally, the Ag-Ag cohesion is maintained according to the OP and BO calculations with only a difference between 2 and 22 % in their values comparing the supported and isolated cluster. In consequence, this analysis confirms that the use of an epoxy group is important for the attachment of the Cd-Ag clusters as it is for Agn clusters [37]. Moreover, attending to the fact that the adsorption of Cd yields a change in the band gap of the system, it allows proposing Agn/E-Cor systems as potential candidates for the detection of Cd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frontier orbital, band gap, work function and electron affinity chemical descriptors analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
A frontier molecular orbital analysis was also carried out, for the most stable systems. Figs. 6 and 7
summarize the results obtained for Ag1/Cor and Ag1/E-Cor systems before and after the Cd adsorption with periodic extended calculations with a plane wave basis set. Additionally, Figure S5 and S6 show the HOMO and LUMO for Agn/Cor and Agn/E-Cor (n = 2,3) before the Cd adsorption, and Figure S7 and S8 after the Cd adsorption.

Based on Figs. 6 and 7, the Ag and Cd atoms have mainly a symmetric spherical form, indicating that the interaction between these atoms should occur mainly through s orbitals. The adsorption of Cd does not generate LUMO states at this isosurface value when the interaction is produced over coronene. Instead, when the silver interacts with the epoxy group, it has localized LUMO states before and after the Cd adsorption, as well as the Cd atom. This can be associated with charge transfer processes, yielding Ag and Cd atoms positively charged. Similar patterns were observed on the Figures S5, S6, S7 and S8. However, for the coronene substrate, when the silver cluster has two or three atoms, LUMO states are probably related to the interaction between them before the Cd adsorption, as it is also reflected in the HOMO states localized on silver atoms. Moreover, the substrate LUMO states do not appear to be reduced as would occur if the silver cluster interacts with the coronene substrate. With the epoxy group the behavior is different for Ag2 and Ag3 clusters; there are still the LUMO states for Ag3 before the Cd adsorption, but at a lesser level, probably because these states are altered by the interaction between silver and oxygen. Subsequently, when the Cd adsorption occurs, in both substrates, part of the LUMO states on Ag atoms disappear and in some cases the presence of LUMO states on Cd atoms is detected, suggesting a charge transfer between these atoms.

In addition, Fig. 8 shows the same results after the Cd adsorption including HOMO-1 and LUMO + 1 from the calculations developed with a cluster approach and double-zeta basis set. Similarly, in the supplementary material we present the results obtained for the all other most stable systems after the Cd adsorption. Figure S9 and S10 show the HOMO, LUMO, HOMO-1 and LUMO + 1 for Cd/Agn/Cor and Cd/Agn/E-Cor (n = 2,3).

From the comparison between Fig. 8 and Fig. 7, it can be appreciated how the use of a different approach and different bases lead to extremely similar HOMO LUMO isosurfaces, regarding the shape and location of the isosurfaces. It is observed that LUMO states are on substrates and HOMO spherical form on Cd and Ag atoms for coronene, while HOMO and LUMO states concentrate on Cd and Ag atoms for epoxy coronene. This similarity is also observed in the other systems for HOMO and LUMO states comparing results obtained by both approaches, as can be observed in Figures S7 to S10. Additionally, in Fig. 8, the subsequent levels (HOMO-1 and LUMO + 1) are mainly related to the C atoms of the substrate, either for coronene or functionalized coronene. This behavior is the same for systems with Ag2 clusters (Figure S9). Only for the systems with the Ag3 cluster, there is some contribution to these states from Cd and Ag atoms (Figure S10), probably related to dispersion in energy as a consequence of the aggregation of atoms. Attending to the fact that the major reactivity refers to states around the Fermi level, these last results evidence that there is interaction between Cd and Ag atoms and also between the latter and O atom as previously stated, for that reason their states belong mainly to the frontier orbitals.

The Cd adsorption capabilities of the studied structures were analyzed also through other types of parameters. In this sense, the energy gap and work function were obtained before and after the Cd adsorption. The changes in these properties were denoted in absolute values for
Δ
E g
and in percentage for %
Δ
E g
and %
Φ
, as in previous works [59,60], in Table 6 . In the results achieved with an unrestricted approach, these calculations were performed for orbitals with spin up and down, represented as α and β respectively.

Table 6 makes it clear that in most of the systems, there is a change in the band gap after the adsorption of a Cd atom, with changes higher than 30 %. In consequence, because the electrical conductivity increases with the decrease of
E g
, this change can lead to a modification in the conductivity that can be used to detect the presence of Cd. The other parameter used to evaluate the electronic sensitivity of hybrid Ag@Cdots structures is the work function sensors property. The adsorption of a molecule or an atom that modifies the
Φ
value of the sensor, can conduct to an electrical signal by changing the gate voltage, which would be useful for chemical recognition [59,60]. In Table 6, this parameter also changes after the Cd adsorption. However, the percentage of variation is lower than the one experimented in the band gap, which conducts to a more unlikely possible application of hybrid Ag@Cdots structures as
Φ
-type sensors.

Finally, the computed chemical hardness, chemical potential and electronegativity were analyzed to complement the analyte sensing performance evaluation, as suggested by other authors in the study of the material‘s ability for the adsorption or sensing of different chemical compounds [59,60]. The obtained values of these chemical descriptors for the structures before and after the Cd adsorption are summarized in Table 7 .

According to these last results, regarding chemical hardness, it can be observed that, before the Cd adsorption, the structures functionalized with epoxy group (Agn/E-Cor) are softer than those non functionalized (Agn/Cor). This means that the former are more likely to modify its electronic structure through charge transfer, and in consequence have more ability to interact with other chemical species. Moreover, the chemical potential values exhibit a general increment after the adsorption of Cd for each structure, which indicates the stabilization after the Cd adsorption (in systems with open shell structures this occurs sometimes for one of the spins).

The study of the systems could be performed using periodic and cluster approaches in a complementary way, taking the strength of both methods in a harmonized fashion. Employing this combined methodology, the adsorption of Cd on silver clusters (n = 1–3) supported on Cdots surfaces could be investigated employing a coronene (with and without an epoxy group) model through DFT calculations, studying carefully the stability and electronic structure through different theoretical techniques. Furthermore, electronic sensitivity parameters were calculated to infer the performance of the hybrid Ag@Cdots structures as Cd sensors.

The ability of supported silver to capture Cd was shown through different types of calculations, demonstrating that the adsorbed Cd on the silver cluster is stable with a charge transfer from Cd to the substrate leading to a positive Cd. Moreover, the reactivity of Cd and silver was inferred through bond orders, overlap population and frontier orbital analysis, concluding that an orbital overlap between Cd and Ag occurs leading to a Cd chemical adsorption. According to HOMO and LUMO states, the Cd interacts with silver mainly through the s orbital. To keep the metallic cluster attached to the substrate, the role of oxygenated functional groups is important through the bond between silver and oxygen, being the functionalized structure chemically softer than the non-functionalized one. This last property enhances the Cd adsorption.

The sensitivity of the hybrid Ag@Cdots structures band gap to the adsorption of Cd was shown through DOS and frontier orbitals calculations, which leads to the potential application of these structures for Cd detection. Also, the capabilities of this composite material as a
Φ
-type sensor were evaluated, observing a low trend towards this type of sensor mechanism.
